narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20 and a family from a high-income bracket, you've put together a thoughtful application to law school, including your LSAT score of 33 and plans to attend full-time. The admissions model we use evaluates applicants based on patterns from past students' paths to the bar exam, predicting whether someone is likely to pass on the first try. For you, it estimated a 41.3% chance of failing the bar exam, which crossed our threshold for rejection to ensure we admit those with the strongest predicted odds of success. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, which is notably lower than the typical 5th or 6th decile—suggesting your later academic performance in school raised concerns about sustaining the rigor needed for law. On a more positive note, your high family income stood out strongly as a protective factor, pulling the prediction toward bar passage more than any other positive influence, likely reflecting resources that help many students like you succeed. Your LSAT score, while below the average of around 37, still contributed helpfully toward a passing prediction, as did your near-average undergrad GPA. Your Year 1 decile of 3rd had a smaller but negative nudge toward failure risk, while your full-time enrollment plans offered a minor boost. Compared to typical students, your early grades and family background align well with those who pass, but the sharp drop in your later school performance was the standout red flag that tipped the scales. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a supportive background—many in similar situations thrive with targeted improvements. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to push closer to 37 or higher, strengthening your later academic record through additional coursework or post-bac programs, and reapplying next cycle with an updated profile that addresses these key areas. We're rooting for you to turn this around and join the ranks of successful lawyers.
